basics.name: Ethan Youmans
basics.title: Software Engineer
basics.subtitle: system architecture • mobile apps • full stack • machine learning

about: I build systems that are meant to be understood and extended.
From user experience to real-time pipelines and OS-integrated tools,
I focus on turning structured ideas into modular software with clear, inspectable behavior.
I prefer explicit design over black-box abstraction, especially in systems that grow over time.

projects.name: Crouton iOS Network Logger
projects.description:
Crouton is an open-source iOS network logging system that captures and records device-level network traffic using Apple’s Network Extensions framework.

 I led a team of five students in designing and implementing the core system architecture for capturing, structuring, and exposing network events from the OS-level logging layer to the application frontend.

 The system transforms raw network activity into structured, queryable records to enable consistent analysis and visualization of device traffic in real time.

 This project was developed as part of a senior capstone (SALADS), focused on building systems for detecting patterns of digital stalking and harassment.

projects.name: Chess Background System
projects.description:
A real-time rule-based chess simulation system that runs continuously in the background and renders live gameplay directly to the desktop wallpaper.

 Instead of using a traditional search-based chess engine, I designed a fully interpretable decision system built around modular conditions and filters that define behavior through explicit, human-readable rules.

 The system operates as a decision pipeline: it evaluates all legal moves, applies rule-based constraints in sequence, and selects actions dynamically. Game state is persisted through a backend database, enabling long-running matches across sessions.

 A custom rendering pipeline converts each board state into an image and updates the Windows desktop wallpaper in real time, producing a continuously evolving simulation of gameplay.

 This project explores alternative rule-based system design by prioritizing interpretability, modularity, and full-stack system integration over traditional engine-based evaluation methods.

projects.name: CLI Tool for Real-time Location Access Detection
projects.description:
This project is a command-line system for real-time detection of applications accessing user location data, developed as part of the SALADS system.

 I designed the system architecture and feature extraction pipeline for processing network flow data, building a lightweight machine learning model trained on packet metadata and inter-arrival timing rather than raw payload inspection.

 The system performs high-throughput analysis of network traffic in real time, processing up to 250k packets per second on a laptop GPU, making it suitable for continuous monitoring workloads.

 This project serves as a proof-of-concept for scalable, privacy-sensitive network telemetry analysis, with future work focused on integration into mobile and OS-level environments.

projects.name: Sudoku Coloring
projects.description:
I implemented a Sudoku solver by modeling the puzzle as a graph coloring problem, where each cell is represented as a vertex and edges encode Sudoku constraints between cells and candidate values.

 I designed and implemented two solving strategies on top of this representation. The first is a greedy heuristic-based approach that prioritizes constraint satisfaction but fails on full 9×9 complexity cases.

 The second approach is inspired by Wave Function Collapse, where each cell maintains a dynamic set of possible values, and the algorithm iteratively collapses possibilities based on constraint propagation and remaining entropy.

 This project explores constraint-based problem solving through graph modeling and heuristic vs propagation-based algorithm design tradeoffs.

projects.name: Hollow Knight: HunterSwap
projects.description:
Built a gameplay extension module on top of existing open-source Hollow Knight multiplayer mods (HKMP and TheHuntIsOn), introducing dynamic player role swapping between “Hunter” and “Speedrunner” states during active online sessions.

 Designed and implemented a state management system responsible for tracking, storing, and switching player roles in real time while maintaining consistency across the underlying multiplayer framework.

 The system integrates with existing networking and gameplay architectures to extend core mod functionality without modifying base frameworks, enabling a new competitive “tag-speedrun” game mode where player roles can change dynamically during play.

 This project extends community-built mod infrastructure and is currently in progress, with additional features and polish planned prior to release.

details: I worked as an undergraduate research assistant at FICS, focusing on reverse engineering and network analysis of mobile location sharing applications. My primary responsibilities included developing experiments to analyze network traffic patterns and identifying potential privacy vulnerabilities in these applications.
